+{"id":"br-1y8ym","title":"Archive git maintenance: background repack + am doctor pack-archive CLI","description":"## Why

After the 2026-04-19 archive-health incident, PR #A added `gc.auto=256` to the
archive's git config so auto-repack fires during normal commit paths. That
stops fresh loose-object accumulation, but it does not proactively clean up
the existing backlog on long-running servers — and some deployments may not
hit the auto-gc threshold often.

## What

Two companion pieces:

1. `mcp_agent_mail_server::maintenance` module
   - Spawns a background task that runs once at startup (after a short delay
     so it doesn't interfere with cold-start probes) and then every 30
     minutes.
   - Invokes `git maintenance run --task=loose-objects --task=incremental-repack`
     on the archive's `.git`, with a low `ionice`/`nice` so it doesn't
     compete with live request handling.
   - Logs kept/removed/size-reclaimed numbers at info.
   - Respects `AM_ARCHIVE_MAINTENANCE_INTERVAL_SECS` (default 1800) and
     `AM_ARCHIVE_MAINTENANCE_DISABLED=1` as env overrides.

2. `am doctor pack-archive` CLI subcommand
   - One-shot manual invocation that runs the same maintenance cycle.
   - Useful after big bulk imports or incident recovery.
   - Prints a table: before/after loose count, pack count, disk use.

## Acceptance

- New unit tests for the maintenance scheduler (interval boundary, disable
  flag, error handling).
- Manual test: on a staging archive with 10k+ loose objects, observe
  auto-cleanup within one maintenance cycle.
- No regression in existing archive commit throughput (benchmark the write
  path during active maintenance — expect <5% overhead).

## Touchpoints

- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/lib.rs` — register new mod + background
  thread registration near `ack_ttl::start` / `cleanup::start`.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/maintenance.rs` — new module.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/lib.rs` — add `doctor pack-archive`
  subcommand, wire to the same maintenance function (sync variant).
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/config.rs` — two new env var readers.

## Out of scope

- Rewriting the archive format (that's bead F).
- Any change to how commits are created.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-19T23:58:18.506761150Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:16:06.260883308Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["archive","gc","performance"]}
 {"id":"br-1yclw","title":"T6.1: File reservation error messages parity (5 errors)","description":"5 file reservation errors: EMPTY_PATHS (paths list cannot be empty), FILE_RESERVATION_CONFLICT (on message write), NOT_FOUND (force-release), RESERVATION_ACTIVE (force-release refused), concurrent release (unable to evaluate). All messages, error codes, and data payloads must match exactly.","notes":"3 of 5 error scenarios verified matching: EMPTY_PATHS (identical), NOT_FOUND (fixed extra project key in data), RESERVATION_ACTIVE (identical). FILE_RESERVATION_CONFLICT is a feature gap (enforcement not implemented in Rust send_message — tracked separately). 'concurrent release' error does not exist in Python codebase.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-15T02:04:32.698471840Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-15T04:35:11.167498419Z","closed_at":"2026-02-15T04:35:11.167433908Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["doc-parity"]}
 {"id":"br-1z66","title":"T2.1: Audit core::flake_triage module vs bash script — identify gaps and reusable logic","description":"## Objective
Establish a precise parity map between `core::flake_triage` capabilities and legacy `scripts/flake_triage.sh` behavior.

## Work
- Inventory existing Rust-side logic and identify script-only behavior gaps.
- Classify each gap as: implement now, intentional delta, or no-longer-needed behavior.
- Record migration decisions with rationale so implementation tasks can execute without ambiguity.

## Deliverable
A concrete parity/gap audit that de-risks implementation and prevents accidental behavior loss.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Defines explicit inputs, outputs, invariants, error taxonomy, and exit-code behavior for downstream implementation.
- Captures parity mapping to legacy behavior, including intentional deviations and technical rationale.
- Includes a concrete validation strategy (unit + integration + e2e) and detailed logging/artifact expectations for downstream tasks.
- Is self-contained and executable without referring back to external markdown plan documents.
- Leaves no ambiguous requirements that would permit incompatible implementations.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:24:29.590143573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T04:43:16.727194628Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T04:43:16.727166936Z","close_reason":"Audit complete by RubyPrairie. Gap analysis posted as comment. 3 gaps identified: artifact scanning, failure reproduction, subprocess multi-seed. All downstream beads (br-36xx, br-154k, br-1kk7) map cleanly.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":263,"issue_id":"br-1z66","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T2.1: Audit core::flake_triage Module vs Bash Script

## What to do
Read and compare two implementations of flake triage:
1. crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/flake_triage.rs (existing Rust module)
2. scripts/flake_triage.sh (bash script being replaced)

## Goals of the audit
1. **Feature gap analysis**: What does the bash script do that the Rust module doesn't?
   Common gaps: artifact scanning, multi-seed detection, CLI integration.
2. **Reusable logic identification**: What functions/types in flake_triage.rs can be
   directly reused by the new CLI subcommand?
3. **Data model gaps**: Does the Rust module define the right structs for:
   - Failure artifacts (test name, seed, error message, timestamp)
   - Flake detection results (pass count, fail count, is_flaky flag)
   - Scan results (list of artifacts found, grouped by test)
4. **Interface recommendations**: What public API does flake_triage.rs need to export
   for the CLI to consume? Document needed additions.

## Deliverable
A comment on this bead summarizing:
- Reusable: [list of functions/types to reuse]
- Missing: [list of capabilities to add]
- Suggested public API for CLI consumption

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/flake_triage.rs (read)
scripts/flake_triage.sh (read)

","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:29:38Z"},{"id":265,"issue_id":"br-1z66","author":"RubyPrairie","text":"# Audit: core::flake_triage.rs vs scripts/flake_triage.sh

## Auditor: RubyPrairie (claude-code/opus-4.6)

---

## Rust Module (flake_triage.rs — 967 lines, 25+ tests)

### Capabilities:
1. **FailureContext** struct: full capture (test_name, harness_seed, e2e_seed, failure_message, failure_ts, repro_command, repro_context, env_snapshot with redaction, rss_kb, uptime_secs, category, notes). Serialize/deserialize + write_artifact().
2. **FlakeReport** struct: multi-run outcomes (RunOutcome), 4 verdicts (Stable/DeterministicFailure/Flaky/Inconclusive), failure_histogram, failing_seeds, remediation text, write_artifact().
3. **run_with_seeds()**: closure-based multi-seed runner. DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS (17 values).
4. **classify_failure()**: pattern-matching for Timing/Contention/CiEnvironment/Assertion/Unknown. CI env awareness.
5. **capture_env_snapshot()**: prefix-filtered capture with secret redaction.
6. **System info**: read_rss_kb() (Linux /proc), read_uptime_secs() (OnceLock Instant).

## Bash Script (flake_triage.sh — 193 lines)

### Capabilities:
1. **Reproduce from artifact** (default mode): reads failure_context.json via python3, displays summary, interactive y/N confirmation, executes repro_command via eval.
2. **Scan for failures** (--scan [dir]): walks directory tree with find -name failure_context.json, displays numbered list with timestamp/name/category/path.
3. **Multi-seed run** (--multi-seed test N): same 17-seed corpus, extends with RANDOM if N>17, runs cargo test subprocess per seed, colored PASS/FAIL per seed, verdict (STABLE/ALWAYS FAILS/FLAKY with rate).

---

## Gap Analysis

### MISSING from Rust (bash has it):

1. **Artifact scanning** — walk directory tree, find failure_context.json files, display summary list with timestamps. (Needed for CLI --scan / downstream br-36xx)
2. **Failure reproduction from artifact** — read JSON artifact, display context, exec repro command. (Needed for CLI reproduce / downstream br-1kk7)
3. **Subprocess-based multi-seed** — spawn cargo test as subprocess per seed. Rust run_with_seeds() uses closures (in-process only). CLI needs subprocess mode for full binary startup + isolation. (Needed for downstream br-154k)
4. **Random seed extension** — bash extends seeds with RANDOM when N > 17. Rust has fixed DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS only.
5. **FailureContext::from_file()** — reading/deserializing existing artifacts (Rust can write but not read back).

### INTENTIONAL DELTAS (Rust is better, keep as-is):

- No python3 dependency (native serde_json)
- In-process closure-based multi-seed for unit tests — keep, plus add subprocess for CLI
- Auto-classification is Rust-side (bash reads category from artifact)
- Environment capture + system metrics are Rust enrichment — bash lacks these

### REUSABLE (no changes needed):

- FailureContext struct + capture() + with_repro() + add_note() + write_artifact()
- FlakeReport struct + from_runs() (verdict, histogram, remediation)
- run_with_seeds() function + DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS
- classify_failure() function (all 6 categories)
- capture_env_snapshot() with secret redaction
- read_rss_kb(), read_uptime_secs()
- RunOutcome, FlakeVerdict, FailureCategory enums

### ADDITIONS NEEDED (suggested public API for CLI):

```rust
/// Read a failure context from a JSON artifact file.
pub fn read_artifact(path: &Path) -> Result<FailureContext, std::io::Error>;

/// Scan a directory tree for failure_context.json artifacts.
pub fn scan_artifacts(dir: &Path) -> Vec<ScannedArtifact>;

/// A scanned failure artifact with file location and summary.
pub struct ScannedArtifact {
    pub path: PathBuf,
    pub context: FailureContext,
}

/// Run a test as a subprocess with multiple seeds.
/// Unlike run_with_seeds() (closures), this spawns cargo test.
pub fn run_multi_seed_subprocess(
    test_name: &str,
    seeds: &[u64],
    extra_packages: &[String],
) -> FlakeReport;

/// Extend seed corpus with random values (for N > DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS.len()).
pub fn extend_seeds(base: &[u64], target_count: usize) -> Vec<u64>;
```

## Summary

The Rust module is a solid foundation with richer data models and classification than the bash script. The main gaps are:
- **Read-side operations** (scan, reproduce) — bash is a CLI tool, Rust is a library
- **Subprocess execution** — needed for CLI integration
- **Seed extension** — minor ergonomic gap

All 3 downstream beads (br-36xx, br-154k, br-1kk7) map cleanly to the identified gaps.

 {"id":"br-1zvl","title":"T3.8: Add deprecation notice header in scripts/bench_cli.sh pointing to am bench","description":"## Objective

- Operator-facing troubleshooting guidance includes where to find logs/artifacts and how to reproduce failures quickly.

- Includes a verification checklist so future maintainers can re-audit correctness without external plan documents.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WindyLynx","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:24:49.237364029Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T04:21:01.095258042Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T04:21:01.095238776Z","close_reason":"Completed: bench_cli shim deprecated with native am bench mapping and docs cleanup","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1zvl","depends_on_id":"br-2x5p4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-11T23:56:06Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":266,"issue_id":"br-1zvl","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T3.8: Add Deprecation Notice in scripts/bench_cli.sh

Add deprecation banner and runtime warning to scripts/bench_cli.sh.

## Changes
1. Banner after shebang:

```bash
# DEPRECATED: Use `am bench` instead.
# Native Rust implementation — no hyperfine or python3 required.

```

2. Runtime warning:
```bash
echo "WARNING: scripts/bench_cli.sh is deprecated. Use 'am bench' instead." >&2
```

## DO NOT delete the script. Keep working for backward compatibility.

## Location
scripts/bench_cli.sh (top of file)

 {"id":"br-a2k3h.6","title":"Conformance 6: CI check — new tool requires new fixture (fail fast on drift)","description":"# Conformance task 6: CI drift prevention

## Preconditions

- br-a2k3h.2 + br-a2k3h.3 + br-a2k3h.4 closed

## Scope

Add a CI check (either a dedicated test in the conformance crate, or a CI workflow step) that fails if a new tool lands in TOOL_CLUSTER_MAP without a corresponding fixture.

## Two implementation options

### Option A: test-driven
Add a Rust test that:
1. Loads the authoritative TOOL_CLUSTER_MAP
2. Loads all fixtures (Python-parity + Rust-native)
3. Asserts every tool in the map has at least one fixture case
4. Asserts every fixture-referenced tool exists in the map

```rust
#[test]
fn every_tool_has_a_fixture() {
    let tools = load_tool_cluster_map();
    let fixtures = load_all_fixtures();
    for tool in tools {
        assert!(fixtures.covers(&tool), "Tool {} has no conformance fixture — add one under fixtures/", tool);
    }
}
```

### Option B: CI-script-driven
Add a shell script in `scripts/conformance_coverage_check.sh` that runs the same check and is invoked from `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

Recommended: Option A — keeps the check close to the code and runs on every developer machine.

## Resource coverage

Same check but for resource templates: every template registered in `resources.rs` must have a fixture OR an explicit Rust-native classification.

## Definition of done

- Check committed
- Deliberately adding a new tool without fixture fails CI
- Adding a fixture without a corresponding tool also fails CI (symmetric drift guard)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:25:26.056941173Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T10:46:19.789Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T10:46:19.789Z","close_reason":"Completed: added docs_drift_ci guard for tool and resource drift.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","conformance","drift-prevention"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:25:26.056941173Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:32.939035925Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:33.577686588Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.6","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:26:34.291389069Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a2k3h.7","title":"Conformance 7: fixture regeneration pipeline — auto-refresh Python-parity fixtures on reference update","description":"# Conformance 7: Fixture regeneration automation

## Why this bead exists

The Python reference implementation (mcp_agent_mail Python repo) evolves independently. When upstream changes a tool's output shape — e.g., renames a field, adds a new optional field, changes error formatting — Rust's conformance tests will fail. Each such drift requires:
1. Running upstream Python to regenerate fixtures
2. Diffing old vs new fixtures
3. Reviewing the delta to decide: upstream bug (report it), intentional Python change (match), or Rust divergence (fix Rust)

Today this is manual and slow. Automate it.

## Scope

### `scripts/regen_python_parity_fixtures.sh`

A script that:
1. Clones/pulls the Python reference repo (known upstream URL)
2. Installs/runs the Python reference in a sandbox
3. Drives it with the canonical fixture scenario catalog (list of tool calls + inputs, defined in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-conformance/tests/conformance/fixtures/scenario_catalog.json`)
4. Captures outputs and writes them as fresh fixtures
5. Diffs new fixtures against existing; emits a summary report of shape changes

### CI integration

A **weekly scheduled GitHub Actions workflow** that runs the regeneration and opens a PR with the new fixtures if there are changes. The PR description auto-classifies:
- New fixture (upstream added a tool we don't have): investigate + decide to port
- Shape changed (field renamed): investigate; may be upstream bug or intentional
- Error format changed: usually upstream bug-fix; match
- No changes: close PR / skip

### Classification heuristics

To reduce review burden:
- **Trivial (auto-merge candidate)**: new optional field added, no removal, no type changes
- **Needs review**: field removed, field renamed, type narrowed, error message changed
- **Blocker**: breaking shape change that would make Rust incompatible

## Test plan

- Run the script manually, verify it produces the expected fixture set
- Force an upstream change (e.g., add a debug field), verify the diff report surfaces it
- Run on a scenario with no changes, verify it exits cleanly with "no changes"
- CI workflow tested on a test branch before enabling on main

## Logging requirements

- Every regeneration run produces a log: upstream commit SHA, scenarios exercised, fixtures updated, time taken
- Logs are committed alongside the fixture update PR (not just CI artifacts) — so future blame can trace "when did we refresh against upstream?"

## Non-goals

- Not writing Python changes ourselves (upstream is their own repo)
- Not auto-merging (always require human review for conformance changes)
- Not running Python in CI constantly (weekly is enough; fixture changes are low-frequency)

## Dependencies

- Depends on br-a2k3h.1-6 (audit + classification + CI check) — pipeline integrates with the classification scheme and drift guard established in those beads

## Definition of done

- Script works locally (can produce fixtures from upstream)
- CI workflow scheduled and green on a test run
- First auto-generated PR (if any upstream drift exists today) reviewed and merged or intentionally ignored
- Process documented in conformance crate README","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:49:28.922204317Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T10:58:19Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T10:58:19Z","close_reason":"Completed: Python-parity fixture regen lane landed via automation files in 3159dc9f plus conformance README operator docs in d87fa591","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["automation","conformance","python-parity"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:49:28.922204317Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:51:02.092165791Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.7","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:52:00.985491646Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-a2k3h.8","title":"Conformance 8: MCP protocol compliance tests beyond tool/resource fixtures (framing, IDs, errors)","description":"# Conformance 8: MCP protocol compliance

## Why this bead exists

The existing conformance harness exercises tool-level and resource-level output shapes against Python-reference fixtures. What it does NOT explicitly test:

- JSON-RPC request/response **ID echoing** (request_id must be echoed by response; MCP spec requirement)
- **Error shape compliance** (MCP errors have specific `code` / `message` / `data` structure)
- **stdio framing** — MCP stdio transport uses newline-delimited JSON; messages must not be split across frames
- **HTTP transport framing** — single request-response per HTTP call; Content-Length or chunked encoding
- **Protocol version negotiation** — client announces supported versions, server responds
- **Initialization handshake** — MCP's `initialize` → `initialized` flow
- **Notifications** — one-way `notifications/*` messages (no ID, no response)
- **Cancellation** — `notifications/cancelled` handling
- **Malformed input handling** — garbage JSON should produce well-formed JSON-RPC errors, not crashes

If `fastmcp-rust` or the MCP spec evolves, our tool-level fixtures won't catch protocol-layer regressions.

## Scope

Add `crates/mcp-agent-mail-conformance/tests/protocol_compliance.rs` with:

### Test 1: JSON-RPC invariants
- Every response echoes request_id exactly (string OR integer preserved)
- Every response has exactly one of `result` or `error` (never both, never neither)
- Notifications (no `id`) never receive a response

### Test 2: Error shape
- Every error has numeric `code`, string `message`, optional `data`
- Standard JSON-RPC codes respected (-32700 parse error, -32600 invalid request, -32601 method not found, -32602 invalid params, -32603 internal error)
- Method-specific error codes documented + used consistently

### Test 3: Transport framing
- stdio: messages delimited by LF; no CRLF unless explicitly escaped; no mixed framing
- HTTP: Content-Type is `application/json`; single message per request
- Both transports: Unicode in strings preserved byte-exact (UTF-8 roundtrip)

### Test 4: Protocol lifecycle
- `initialize` → `initialized` handshake succeeds
- Protocol version negotiation: client requests latest; server responds with compatible
- Unknown tools/resources return method-not-found error, not crash

### Test 5: Malformed input
- Garbage JSON → parse error with code -32700
- Missing method field → invalid request with code -32600
- Wrong param types → invalid params with code -32602
- Server DOES NOT crash on any of these

### Test 6: Large payloads
- Request bodies near size limits (1MB, 10MB) handled or rejected with clear error
- Streaming responses (if any) don't truncate on limit boundary

### Test 7: Concurrent requests (stdio)
- Multiple requests interleaved; responses match correct request_ids (not serialized to request order)

## Test plan

- Each test parameterized across both stdio and HTTP transports
- Reference upstream `fastmcp-rust` test fixtures where applicable
- Include a version-drift test: pin fastmcp-rust to current version; if upstream bumps major, this test fires

## Logging requirements

```
conformance.protocol.test_start { scenario, transport }
conformance.protocol.invariant_check { scenario, invariant_name, pass, detail }
conformance.protocol.test_complete { scenario, transport, passed, duration }
```

Each failure captures:
- Full request payload
- Full response payload  
- Invariant that failed
- Expected vs actual

## Non-goals

- Not reimplementing the MCP spec (defer to upstream)
- Not implementing optional MCP features (e.g., sampling, prompts) unless the project uses them
- Not testing against multiple MCP implementations (python-reference is our comparison, not third parties)

## Dependencies

- Depends on br-a2k3h.1 (audit) — audit should surface whether these gaps exist today
- Coordinates with br-a2k3h.6 (CI drift-guard) — protocol checks run in CI alongside tool/resource checks

## Definition of done

- Test file + 7 scenarios implemented
- Tests run on both stdio and HTTP
- CI-integrated; fails on regression
- One intentionally-introduced regression test (e.g., a shim that breaks ID echo) verifies the tests actually catch breakage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TopazSummit","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:52:54.026711445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T18:32:31Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T18:32:31Z","close_reason":"Completed: added MCP protocol compliance transport coverage and passing conformance tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","jsonrpc","mcp-protocol","transport"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:52:54.026711445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:53:57.119192194Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-a2k3h.8","depends_on_id":"br-a2k3h.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-18T03:53:57.379867858Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"br-a3g6f","title":"Archive commit batching: coalesce mail events into grouped commits","description":"## Why

Today every mail event (send_message, register_agent, ensure_product,
ensure_project, file_reservation state change, etc.) cuts its own git
commit via `git_add_and_commit` / `commit_paths_with_retry`. On a busy
multi-agent mailbox this produces hundreds of commits per minute — each
one a fresh tree and set of loose objects.

The gc tuning from bead (A) and the maintenance task from bead (E) bound
the *cleanup*, but the commit-rate pressure remains. Batching commits
would reduce loose-object churn 10-100x.

## What

A commit-coalescer service:

- Accept commit requests `{ repo_root, paths, message, author }` via an
  unbounded channel from `write_agent_profile`, `write_message`, etc.
- Batch by `(repo_root, flush-window)` with two triggers:
    - N events since last flush (default 32)
    - T milliseconds since first buffered event (default 1000)
  (both overridable via env: `AM_ARCHIVE_BATCH_EVENTS`,
  `AM_ARCHIVE_BATCH_MS`.)
- On flush, call the existing `commit_paths_with_retry` once with all
  accumulated paths and a grouped message like
  `batch: 17 events (2026-04-19T22:31:15Z – 22:31:16Z)`.
- Graceful shutdown: on SIGTERM, drain the queue (flush with timeout) so
  no events are lost.

## Design notes

- Keep the queue *per-storage-root*. In practice a single server process
  only touches one storage_root, but the abstraction lets multi-tenant
  builds scale.
- Preserve the guarantees the current per-event commit gives: ensure the
  archive is always recoverable (no half-written state).
- Observability: emit histogram of batch sizes so we can verify the
  batching is actually coalescing.

## Out of scope

- Replacing git as the archive format (separate discussion).

## Touchpoints

- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-storage/src/lib.rs` — funnel the existing
  `commit_paths_with_retry` callers through the coalescer.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-storage/src/commit_coalescer.rs` — new module.
- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/config.rs` — two env var readers.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-19T23:58:43.843409025Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:16:30.911499884Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["archive","performance","scalability"]}
 {"id":"br-a6daf","title":"T6.2: Suspicious pattern warning messages parity","description":"3 suspicious pattern warning categories: (1) too-broad patterns (*, **, **/*) with specific advisory message, (2) absolute paths (starting with /) with project-relative hint, (3) very short patterns (<=2 chars with *) with may-match-more warning. Advisory messages must match character-for-character.","notes":"Implemented detect_suspicious_file_reservation() with 3 Python-matching categories: too-broad (*, **, **/*, **/**), absolute paths (/foo), very short (<=2 with *). Wired into file_reservation_paths(). 5 unit tests added.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-15T02:04:32.967772958Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-15T04:48:32.255509600Z","closed_at":"2026-02-15T04:48:32.255404603Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["doc-parity"]}
 {"id":"br-aazao","title":"Verification Coverage Program: real-path unit tests, E2E completeness, and forensic logging","description":"# Verification Coverage Program: Real-Path Unit Tests, E2E Completeness, and Forensic Logging

## Why
The repository already has a large amount of testing surface area, but it would be inaccurate to claim any of the following today:
- full unit coverage across the workspace
- zero reliance on mocks, stubs, fake binaries, fake repos, or synthetic fixtures
- complete end-to-end coverage across every critical workflow and failure mode
- uniformly excellent, information-dense logging and artifact capture across all E2E suites

Current evidence for those gaps is already visible in-tree:
- deterministic stub encoder coverage in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/tests/toon_integration.rs`
- stub embedder / stub engine coverage in search-core and DB search-bridge tests
- stubbed LLM completions in `crates/mcp-agent-mail-tools/src/llm.rs`
- mock release artifacts in `tests/e2e/test_idempotency.sh`
- fake repo / fake lock / synthetic owner-state scenarios in storage tests
- wrapper-style E2E entrypoints where artifact richness and logging density are not guaranteed to be identical suite-to-suite

## Goal
Create a disciplined verification program that:
- maps every important module and workflow to concrete tests
- maximizes real-path coverage rather than simulated-only confidence
- explicitly documents any remaining sanctioned substitutes that cannot yet be removed
- standardizes E2E artifact capture, logging density, and rerun breadcrumbs
- installs CI gates so future contributors cannot quietly regress realism or coverage

## Deliverables
- repo-wide audit of test coverage, realism, and logging quality
- policy for what counts as acceptable test realism and when substitutes are allowed
- burn-down plan for fake-only critical-path tests
- granular unit-coverage closure across crate clusters
- granular E2E matrix closure across transport, workflow, failure, and performance paths
- forensic artifact and logging contract for all E2E suites
- CI/reporting/sign-off layer for maintaining the standard

## Acceptance Criteria
- a future maintainer can answer, with evidence rather than vibes, which critical paths are covered by real-path unit tests, which are covered by E2E, which still rely on sanctioned substitutes, and why
- no critical user-visible or operator-visible path is validated only by ad hoc stubs or thin happy-path E2E wrappers
- failures produce enough structured data to debug without blind reruns
- the resulting bead tree is self-contained enough that implementation can proceed without needing to reconstruct this reasoning from chat history

## Important Notes
This is a repo-wide verification hardening program, not a single test bead. The point is to turn broad but uneven coverage into trustworthy and explainable coverage.","status":"tombstone","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","estimated_minutes":0,"created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.708072106Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035073367Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035073367Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:14.035014998Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"epic","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"br-aazao.1","title":"Verification Coverage 1.1: repo-wide audit of test realism, gaps, and ownership","description":"# Verification Coverage 1.1: Repo-Wide Audit of Test Realism, Gaps, and Ownership

## Why
Before adding more tests, the project needs an explicit inventory of what is already covered, how that coverage is achieved, and where confidence is artificially inflated by substitutes, wrappers, or fixture-only paths.

## Deliverables
- enumerate the workspace by crate, major module cluster, and critical workflow surface
- map each surface to existing unit, property, integration, conformance, and E2E coverage
- classify each test by realism grade:
  - real-path / real dependency
  - deterministic local fixture
  - sanctioned substitute
  - mock / fake / stub
  - untested
- identify which tests are merely contract or formatting tests versus true behavioral tests
- identify ownership / expected home for missing tests so the backlog is implementable
- produce a prioritized gap ledger that distinguishes critical-path gaps from cosmetic gaps

## Acceptance Criteria
- future beads can point back to this issue as the canonical inventory rather than rediscovering the current state
- the audit explicitly calls out present realism exceptions such as stub encoders, stub embedders, stub LLM completions, mock installer artifacts, and fake filesystem/repo state tests
- the audit is specific enough to drive per-subsystem implementation work without hand-wavy “add more tests” follow-ups

## Important Notes
This is not a paper exercise. The output must be actionable enough that later beads can close gaps mechanically.","status":"tombstone","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":240,"created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.997462159Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649697626Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649697626Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:39.649644266Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"task","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["coverage","e2e","repo-wide","testing","unit-tests","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-aazao.1","depends_on_id":"br-aazao","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-18T13:51:02.997462159Z","created_by":"CrimsonSnow","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-xfm18.3","title":"ATC Learning Phase 3: adaptive math stack and safety envelope","description":"# Phase 3: Adaptive Math Stack and Safety Envelope

This phase turns captured experiences into useful adaptation. It covers outcome labeling, sufficient statistics, shrinkage, conformal budgets, regret-bounded policy updates, regime detection, and automatic rollback.

## Deliverables
- deterministic outcome automata
- materialized sufficient-stat rollups
- shrinkage across sparse cohorts
- conformal risk budgets by action family and stratum
- finite-policy adaptation with exploration constraints
- regime detection and safe rollback logic","status":"tombstone","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:22.790664627Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:17.477847709Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:17.477847709Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:17.477796162Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"epic","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact","atc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-xfm18.3","depends_on_id":"br-xfm18","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:22.790664627Z","created_by":"codex","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-xfm18.4","title":"ATC Learning Phase 4: operator surfaces, audit artifacts, and explainability","description":"# Phase 4: Operator Surfaces, Audit Artifacts, and Explainability

This phase makes the learning system inspectable. ATC needs to show not just what it decided, but which experiences are still open, what the regret trend looks like, whether calibration is stressed, what regime is active, and why the current policy is trusted.

## Deliverables

- learning-aware `am robot atc` output
- TUI/system-health visibility for learning state
- periodic policy snapshots and audit summaries
- operator controls and explainability hooks","status":"tombstone","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:31.780793801Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:27.610266699Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:27.610266699Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:27.610211456Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"epic","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact","atc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-xfm18.4","depends_on_id":"br-xfm18","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:31.780793801Z","created_by":"codex","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-xfm18.5","title":"ATC Learning Phase 5: replay, verification, and hardening","description":"# Phase 5: Replay, Verification, and Hardening

This phase proves the learning system is stable, bounded, and correct enough to trust. It adds deterministic replay fixtures, regression/property coverage, live-server scenarios, performance budget tests, remote verification, and a final hardening pass.

## Deliverables
- synthetic scenario corpus and replay harness
- unit/property/regression coverage for learning invariants
- E2E ATC learning tests against a live server
- performance/storage/lock-contention checks
- remote verification and fresh-eyes audit","status":"tombstone","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:40.598727141Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:35.524516700Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:35.524516700Z","source_repo":".","deleted_at":"2026-04-03T01:13:35.524446980Z","deleted_by":"br-rebuild","delete_reason":"rebuild: not in JSONL","original_type":"epic","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-artifact","atc"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-xfm18.5","depends_on_id":"br-xfm18","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-17T19:48:40.598727141Z","created_by":"codex","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-xj5oi","title":"ATC rollup refresh drops compacted history for touched stratum","description":"Attack surface: ATC learning loop + retention/rollup interaction.

Invariant broken:
- docs and code assume rollups outlive compacted raw rows.
- after retention_compact() deletes old terminal rows, a later refresh_rollups() on the same stratum recomputes from surviving raw rows only and overwrites the preserved rollup entry. historical totals silently shrink.

Concrete repro:
1. Insert one resolved liveness/probe experience old enough to compact.
2. refresh_rollups() -> rollup total_count=1,resolved_count=1.
3. retention_compact(..., preserve_rollups=true) -> raw row deleted, rollup still 1/1.
4. Insert a new resolved liveness/probe experience.
5. refresh_rollups() again.
6. Expected: total_count=2,resolved_count=2.
7. Actual: rollup is recomputed from surviving raw rows and drops historical compacted totals.

Local repro harness added in worktree during review: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/atc_queries.rs test name refresh_rollups_can_erase_compacted_history_for_touched_stratum (ignored because bug is known and currently failing).

Impact:
- ATC policy engine can undercount historical outcomes after compaction.
- replay/retention/rollup views diverge, making the learning loop lie about sample size and outcome mix.
- any downstream explain/simulate/policy decisions that trust rollups can be biased low after compaction.

Likely fix direction:
- introduce durable per-stratum compaction watermarks / cumulative baselines, or
- make rollups append-only with delta application instead of absolute recompute from remaining raw rows.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-18T22:10:43.757824273Z","created_by":"NavySummit","updated_at":"2026-04-18T22:10:43.757824273Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["atc","retention","review-r3"]}
+{"id":"br-xj5oi","title":"ATC rollup refresh drops compacted history for touched stratum","description":"Attack surface: ATC learning loop + retention/rollup interaction.

Invariant broken:
- docs and code assume rollups outlive compacted raw rows.
- after retention_compact() deletes old terminal rows, a later refresh_rollups() on the same stratum recomputes from surviving raw rows only and overwrites the preserved rollup entry. historical totals silently shrink.

Concrete repro:
1. Insert one resolved liveness/probe experience old enough to compact.
2. refresh_rollups() -> rollup total_count=1,resolved_count=1.
3. retention_compact(..., preserve_rollups=true) -> raw row deleted, rollup still 1/1.
4. Insert a new resolved liveness/probe experience.
5. refresh_rollups() again.
6. Expected: total_count=2,resolved_count=2.
7. Actual: rollup is recomputed from surviving raw rows and drops historical compacted totals.

Local repro harness added in worktree during review: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/atc_queries.rs test name refresh_rollups_can_erase_compacted_history_for_touched_stratum (ignored because bug is known and currently failing).

Impact:
- ATC policy engine can undercount historical outcomes after compaction.
- replay/retention/rollup views diverge, making the learning loop lie about sample size and outcome mix.
- any downstream explain/simulate/policy decisions that trust rollups can be biased low after compaction.

Likely fix direction:
- introduce durable per-stratum compaction watermarks / cumulative baselines, or
- make rollups append-only with delta application instead of absolute recompute from remaining raw rows.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"IvorySummit","created_at":"2026-04-18T22:10:43.757824273Z","created_by":"NavySummit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:15:55.967352153Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:15:55.966849842Z","close_reason":"Already completed by 6e291c1c and df9b1492; current atc_queries.rs contains compacted rollup baselines plus regression coverage for the repro.","closed_by_session":"IvorySummit","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["atc","retention","review-r3"]}
 {"id":"br-xqs2q","title":"T1: Search cluster tool description parity","description":"Copy the EXACT Python docstring for each tool in the Search cluster into the Rust #[tool(description = "...")] attribute.

 {"id":"br-xuvzq","title":"T13.1: Implement mouse drag-and-drop for messages with drop zone highlighting","description":"Implement mouse-based drag-and-drop for the most common use case: dragging a message
to re-thread it (move to a different thread).

MOUSE DND FLOW:
1. Click and hold on a message in Messages/Threads screen for 200ms -> start drag
2. Ghost of message follows cursor (message subject text, semi-transparent)
3. Hover over thread list -> thread items highlight as valid drop zones
4. Release on a thread -> update message's thread_id via internal tool dispatch
5. Release elsewhere -> cancel, message returns to original position

DROP ZONE RENDERING:
- Valid drop zones get palette.selection_bg background
- Invalid zones get palette.severity_warn border flash
- Currently hovered valid zone gets palette.panel_border_focused border

ERROR HANDLING:
- If tool dispatch fails (e.g., permission error), show error toast
- If dropped on same thread (no change), do nothing silently
- If source message not found, show warning toast

FILES: tui_app.rs (drag state), tui_screens/messages.rs, tui_screens/threads.rs","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Click-and-hold initiates drag after 200ms
- [ ] Ghost message follows cursor
- [ ] Valid drop zones highlight
- [ ] Drop on thread re-threads message
- [ ] Cancel on invalid drop zone

Plan-space hardening additions:

 {"id":"br-xy39","title":"HTTP CORS parity (dev defaults + allowlist)","description":"## Objective
Implement CORS behavior parity for HTTP server.

## Scope
- Env vars: `HTTP_CORS_ENABLED`, `HTTP_CORS_ORIGINS`, `HTTP_CORS_ALLOW_CREDENTIALS`, `HTTP_CORS_ALLOW_METHODS`, `HTTP_CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS`.
- Default: enabled in development, disabled in production (legacy behavior).
- Allowlist handling: empty origins → `*`.

## Tests
- Integration tests for preflight OPTIONS and response headers.
- E2E coverage in HTTP suite.

## Logging/Artifacts
- Capture CORS headers under `tests/artifacts/http/cors/<timestamp>/`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. CORS headers match legacy defaults and configuration.
2.
